1. ## Known Issue: Cross-Origin (OOP) Iframe Content — RESOLVED
2. 
3. 
4. 
5. ### Problem Description
6. 
7. When working with SharePoint-hosted documents (Word Online, Excel Online, PowerPoint Online),
8. 
9. the actual document content renders inside a **cross-origin iframe** from
10. 
11. `*.officeapps.live.com`. Prior to CDP Browser MCP Server v4.12.0, this content was
12. 
13. completely invisible:
14. 
15. 
16. 
17. 1. `page.snapshot` only returned the empty SharePoint shell (6 elements) — the Word Online
18. 
19.    ribbon, document body, and all text were missing
20. 
21. 2. `execute.eval` with `iframe.contentDocument` was blocked by same-origin policy  
22. 
23. 3. Navigating directly to the Word Online URL (`ppc-word-edit.officeapps.live.com/...`)
24. 
25.    broke SSO authentication — showed "Sorry, we ran into a problem" with a Sign in button
26. 
27. 4. `fetch_webpage` also hit the SSO auth wall
28. 
29. 5. Agents attempted workarounds: extracting iframe src from scripts, trying WOPI REST API,
30. 
31.    SharePoint REST API — all failed due to auth token scoping
32. 
33. 
34. 
35. ### Root Cause
36. 
37. Chrome's **Site Isolation** runs cross-origin iframes in separate OS processes (Out-Of-Process
38. 
39. iframes). CDP's `Page.getFrameTree` and `Accessibility.getFullAXTree` only work on
40. 
41. same-process frames. Without `Target.setAutoAttach`, OOP iframes were entirely invisible
42. 
43. to CDP — no snapshot data, no element refs, no JS execution capability.
44. 
45. 
46. 
47. ### Resolution (v4.12.0)
48. 
49. The CDP Browser MCP Server now:
50. 
51. - Calls `Target.setAutoAttach` on every page session to auto-discover OOP iframe targets
52. 
53. - Captures accessibility tree content from cross-origin iframes in `page.snapshot`
54. 
55.   (shown with `[frame N]` prefixes)
56. 
57. - Supports clicking, typing, and interacting with elements inside cross-origin iframes
58. 
59.   via `uid` refs from snapshots
60. 
61. - Supports `execute.call` and `page.content` with `uid` for cross-origin frame elements
62. 
63. - Provides `page.frames` action to list all frames including cross-origin ones
64. 
65. 
66. 
67. ### How to Read SharePoint Word Online Documents Now
68. 
69. 1. Navigate to the SharePoint URL: `page.goto` with the sharepoint.com URL
70. 
71. 2. Wait for load: `page.wait` with timeout ~10000ms
72. 
73. 3. Take snapshot: `page.snapshot` — you will now see `[frame 1]` entries with the
74. 
75.    full Word Online content including ribbon, document body, and all text
76. 
77. 4. Extract text: `execute.call` with `uid` of the "Document Contents" element
78. 
79.    (typically found via the snapshot) and function `(el) => el.innerText`
80. 
81. 5. Or use `page.content` with `uid` to get text/html of specific elements
82. 
83. 
84. 
85. ### Affected Sites
86. 
87. Any page embedding cross-origin iframes:
88. 
89. - SharePoint Word/Excel/PowerPoint Online documents
90. 
91. - OAuth consent screens in iframes  
92. 
93. - Cross-origin payment forms (Stripe, PayPal embeds)
94. 
95. - Embedded third-party widgets from different domains
```

</details>

1. Now regarding the CDP browser limitation for this use case — the interact tool's click action with a selector of iframe always clicks at the iframe center position, ignoring the x/y offsets. And the snapshot action can't traverse into cross-origin iframes. So I couldn't click on specific elements inside the Logic App designer (like the "Code preview" tab on Condition_6). But the screenshot action captures the full page including iframe content, which is what let us verify the Code view.
2.  Now for the **CDP browser iframe limitation** — here's a detailed feature request you can file:
3. 
4. ---
5. 
6. **Feature Request: Cross-Origin Iframe Interaction Support**
7. 
8. **Problem:**
9. The CDP browser MCP server cannot interact with content inside cross-origin sandboxed iframes. The `snapshot` action only captures the parent page's accessibility tree (stopping at `IframePresentational` ref nodes). The `interact` action with `selector: "iframe"` clicks at the iframe's center regardless of `x`/`y` parameters. The `execute` action's `eval` runs in the parent context, not the iframe's JavaScript context.
10. 
11. **Impact:**
12. Azure Portal, Power Platform, and many enterprise SaaS applications render their primary UI inside cross-origin sandboxed iframes (e.g., `sandbox-1.reactblade-ms.portal.azure.net`). This makes the CDP browser effectively unable to:
13. - Read any element text/attributes inside these UIs
14. - Click on specific buttons/tabs/fields inside them
15. - Fill form inputs or interact with rich editors
16. - Take element-level snapshots of the actual application UI
17. 
18. The `screenshot` action DOES capture the full rendered page including iframe pixels (confirmed working), but there's no way to interact with what's visible.
19. 
20. **Root Cause:**
21. CDP's `Runtime.evaluate` and DOM methods execute in a specific `executionContextId`. Cross-origin iframes create separate execution contexts. The current implementation only uses the main frame's context.
22. 
23. **Proposed Solution:**
24. 1. **`interact` action**: Support `x`/`y` coordinate-based clicking via CDP's `Input.dispatchMouseEvent` which operates at the browser viewport level, bypassing frame boundaries. This already works for screenshots (which use `Page.captureScreenshot`).
25. 
26. 2. **`execute` action**: Add an optional `frameId` or `frameSelector` parameter. Use CDP's `Page.getFrameTree` to enumerate all frames, then `Runtime.evaluate` with the correct `contextId` for the target frame.
27. 
28. 3. **`snapshot` action**: Traverse into iframe execution contexts using `Page.getFrameTree` + `DOM.getDocument` per frame, then merge the accessibility trees.
29. 
30. **Diagnostic Evidence:**
31. 
32. Page structure showing the limitation:
33. ```
34. - RootWebArea "MaSHTest-GPI-..." [ref=1]
35.   - banner "Microsoft Azure" [ref=6]
36.     - (portal chrome - buttons, search, etc)
37.   - main [ref=46]
38.     - region "MaSHTest-GPI-..." [ref=56]
39.       - heading "MaSHTest-GPI-..." [ref=59]
40.       - IframePresentational [ref=76]    ← ALL app content is INSIDE here
41.                                           ← snapshot/interact STOPS here
42. ```
43. 
44. The iframe details:
45. - **src**: `https://sandbox-1.reactblade-ms.portal.azure.net/React/Index?...`
46. - **size**: 1874x824 (full viewport)
47. - **origin**: Different from parent (`ms.portal.azure.com` vs `sandbox-1.reactblade-ms.portal.azure.net`)
48. 
49. What works today:
50. - `page.screenshot` — captures full page including iframe pixels
51. - `execute.eval` — runs in parent frame only
52. - `interact.click` with `selector: "iframe"` — clicks iframe center, ignores custom coordinates
53. 
54. What's needed:
55. - `interact.click` with `x`/`y` coordinates relative to viewport (using `Input.dispatchMouseEvent`)
56. - `execute.eval` targeting a specific iframe context
57. - `snapshot` that traverses into iframes
58. 
59. **Sample CDP Protocol Calls:**
60. 
61. For coordinate-based clicking (bypasses iframe boundaries):
62. ```javascript
63. // CDP Input.dispatchMouseEvent works at viewport level
64. await cdp.send('Input.dispatchMouseEvent', {
65.   type: 'mousePressed', x: 684, y: 138, button: 'left', clickCount: 1
66. });
67. await cdp.send('Input.dispatchMouseEvent', {
68.   type: 'mouseReleased', x: 684, y: 138, button: 'left'
69. });
70. ```
71. 
72. For iframe-context execution:
73. ```javascript
74. // Get all frames
75. const {frameTree} = await cdp.send('Page.getFrameTree');
76. // Find the iframe by URL pattern
77. const targetFrame = findFrame(frameTree, f => f.url.includes('reactblade'));
78. // Create isolated world or use existing context
79. const {executionContextId} = await cdp.send('Page.createIsolatedWorld', {
80.   frameId: targetFrame.frame.id
81. });
82. // Execute in iframe context
83. await cdp.send('Runtime.evaluate', {
84.   expression: 'document.querySelectorAll("button").length',
85.   contextId: executionContextId
86. });
87. ```
88. 
89. 
90. User: ```

182. Everything works perfectly now. The issue was simply that **the server wasn't connected to any browser instance**. The CDP browser MCP server doesn't auto-reconnect between sessions — when a new agent session starts, it needs to call `browser → connect` first.
183. 
184. **Root cause:** The agent that failed was trying to use `tabs` (action: "new") without first connecting via `browser → connect → Edge`. The server was in a disconnected state.
185. 
186. **Fix for the other agent:** It just needs to run `browser.connect(instance: "Edge")` before any other CDP tool call. Edge on port 9222 is fully accessible and working — 8 tabs visible, mashtest already open.

136. 
137. ```
138. 1. # Check Responses - Complete Mail & Message Checking Workflow
139. 2. 
140. 3. ## READ THIS EVERY TIME. RE-READ AFTER EVERY ACTION.
141. 4. 
142. 5. > Version: 1.0 | Created: 2026-03-30
143. 6. > This is the single source of truth for checking application responses across ALL platforms.
144. 7. > Every agent MUST read this file at the start. No shortcuts. No "I already know the plan." Read it again.
145. 8. 
146. 9. ---
147. 10. 
148. 11. ## Quick Reference - Key Files
149. 12. 
150. 13. | File | What | When to read |
151. 14. |---|---|---|
152. 15. | `candidate/current-status.json` | Personal info, email, phone | Before every session |
153. 16. | `data/tracker-active.json` | All active/pending applications | Cross-reference with responses |
154. 17. | `data/tracker-closed.json` | Rejected/no-response/withdrawn | Reference for already-closed apps |
155. 18. | `data/manual-tasks.md` | Items needing manual action | Update with new items found |
156. 19. | `candidate/accounts.json` | Login credentials | When logging into platforms |
157. 20. | `.github/guides/platform-guide.md` | Platform-specific procedures | How to navigate each site |
158. 21. | `prompts/plan-jobApplicationWorkflow.prompt.md` | Master workflow plan (v3.4) | For full rules, failure patterns, tracker format |
159. 22. | `prompts/plan-jobApplicationWorkflow.prompt.md` | Master workflow plan | For tracker/folder rules |
160. 23. 
161. 24. ---
162. 25. 
163. 26. ## FAILURE PATTERNS - MISTAKES TO NEVER REPEAT
164. 27. 
165. 28. These are real mistakes from past sessions. Read every one. Don't repeat them.
166. 29. 
167. 30. ### 1. Used wrong Gmail account
168. 31. Agent composed and sent outreach emails from tushar.agarwal0901@gmail.com (u/0, personal) instead of t.ushara0901@gmail.com (u/1, job account). The personal account tab was open and the agent used it without checking.
169. 32. **FIX**: ALWAYS verify the Gmail URL contains `/mail/u/1/` not `/mail/u/0/`. Before composing any email, check the URL. The compose URL is: `https://mail.google.com/mail/u/1/?view=cm&fs=1&to=...`
170. 33. 
171. 34. ### 2. Shallow Gmail checks - missed emails
172. 35. Agents searched Gmail with broad queries, scanned subject lines, and missed specific rejection emails, recruiter replies, and assessment invites buried in threads.
173. 36. **FIX**: Open each unread email ONE BY ONE. Read the FULL content. Don't rely on subject line scanning. Check ALL labels including "Dot 1".
174. 37. 
175. 38. ### 3. LinkedIn click handlers don't respond to CDP interact
176. 39. LinkedIn's Ember.js event handlers don't respond to CDP coordinate-based mouse events on message list items and some conversation elements.
177. 40. **FIX**: Use `el.click()` via JS execute (mcp_cdp-browser_execute), not CDP `interact` click, for LinkedIn messaging elements.
178. 41. 
179. 42. ### 4. Hirist 403 overlay blocks page
180. 43. Hirist.tech accessibility tree becomes mostly empty due to a 403 error dialog overlay that blocks the underlying page content.
181. 44. **FIX**: Dismiss the overlay first via JS: `document.querySelectorAll('[class*="dialog"], [class*="modal"]').forEach(el => el.remove())` before reading notifications.
182. 45. 
183. 46. ### 5. Hirist /myApplications returns 404
184. 47. The URL hirist.tech/myApplications doesn't exist - feature was removed.
185. 48. **FIX**: Use the notifications bell icon only for status updates on Hirist. Don't try to navigate to /myApplications.
186. 49. 
187. 50. ### 6. Subagent prompts too long cause 400 errors
188. 51. Subagent prompts over ~2500 characters trigger `invalid_request_error: request body is not valid JSON: unexpected EOF`.
189. 52. **FIX**: Keep subagent prompts under 2500 chars. Use bullet points, not paragraphs. Split complex tasks into smaller subagent calls.
190. 53. 
191. 54. ### 7. Tracker updated in bulk corrupted JSON
192. 55. Agents tried to update 20+ tracker entries at once, causing malformed JSON.
193. 56. **FIX**: Update tracker entries one at a time or in small batches (max 5). Validate JSON after every edit with: `python -c "import json; json.load(open('FILE', encoding='utf-8-sig')); print('valid')"`
194. 57. 
195. 58. ### 8. LinkedIn viewport not set - mobile layout
196. 59. Without viewport set, LinkedIn renders mobile layout and messaging/notifications break.
197. 60. **FIX**: ALWAYS set viewport 1920x1080 via `mcp_cdp-browser_emulate` on EACH NEW TAB before navigating to LinkedIn. Viewport is per-tab.
198. 61. 
199. 62. ### 9. No systematic follow-up for stale applications
200. 63. 211+ applications sat in "applied" status for 7+ days with no follow-up or cleanup. No one checked if job listings were still active.
````

200. 63. 211+ applications sat in "applied" status for 7+ days with no follow-up or cleanup. No one checked if job listings were still active.
201. 64. **FIX**: After every response-check session, run `python scripts/check_stale.py` to list stale applications. For 30+ day old "applied" entries, run `python scripts/sweep_stale.py` to move them to no-response.
202. 65. 
203. 66. ### 10. Missed hiring manager information
204. 67. Agents found hiring managers on job pages but didn't save their details or update the tracker.
205. 68. **FIX**: If you discover any hiring team info (LinkedIn notifications "application viewed by", recruiter names in emails), update the tracker entry's hiringManager field AND create/update HiringManagerOutreach.md in the application folder.
206. 69. 
207. 70. ### 11. Google Forms had stale draft values
208. 71. Google Forms auto-saves drafts per Google account. Pre-filled values from previous incomplete sessions were incorrect (e.g., CTC showing 11.5 instead of 12).
209. 72. **FIX**: Before submitting any Google Form discovered via email, verify ALL pre-filled values against candidate/current-status.json.
210. 73. 
211. 74. ### 12. Not updating data/manual-tasks.md
212. 75. After finding new assessments, interviews, or action items in email, agents didn't add them to data/manual-tasks.md.
213. 76. **FIX**: After processing each response, if it requires manual action (assessment, interview, ID verification), immediately add it to data/manual-tasks.md with link, tracker reference, and folder reference.
214. 77. 
215. 78. ### 13. Status changed but folder not moved
216. 79. When marking an application as rejected/no-response, the tracker entry was updated but the folder was left in Applications/ instead of being moved to Applications/.archive/.
217. 80. **FIX**: When closing an application, use `python scripts/archive_closed.py` or manually move the folder AND update the folderPath in the tracker entry.
218. ```

240. 83. 
241. 84. ## THE RULES - NON-NEGOTIABLE
242. 85. 
243. 86. ### Rule 1: Correct Gmail account
244. 87. ALL job email checking MUST use `mail.google.com/mail/u/1/` (t.ushara0901@gmail.com).
245. 88. NEVER use `mail/u/0` (tushar.agarwal0901@gmail.com - personal).
246. 89. Before EVERY Gmail action, visually verify the URL contains `/mail/u/1/`.
247. 90. 
248. 91. ### Rule 2: Read every email individually
249. 92. Do NOT scan subject lines and move on. Open each email. Read the content. Many rejection emails have misleading subjects ("Update on your application" could be rejection or interview). Assessment links are often buried in email bodies.
250. 93. 
251. 94. ### Rule 3: Cross-reference with tracker
252. 95. Every response you find MUST be matched to a tracker entry. Search by company name, role, or job ID in data/tracker-active.json. If the response is for a closed application, check data/tracker-closed.json.
253. 96. 
254. 97. ### Rule 4: Update tracker immediately
255. 98. When you find a response:
256. 99. - **Rejection:** Change status to "rejected", move entry from Active to Closed, move folder to .archive
257. 100. - **Interview invite:** Change status to "interview", add to data/manual-tasks.md
258. 101. - **Assessment link:** Change status to "assessment-completed" or "assignment", add to data/manual-tasks.md
259. 102. - **Shortlisted/profile viewed:** Change status to "shortlisted", add note with date
260. 103. - **Recruiter reply:** Change status to "replied", quote key info in notes
261. 104. - **No response after 30 days:** Change status to "no-response", move to Closed
262. 105. 
263. 106. ### Rule 5: Update data/manual-tasks.md
264. 107. Any item needing manual action (assessment, interview, ID verification, form completion) MUST be added to data/manual-tasks.md with:
265. 108. - Status (assessment/in-progress/monitoring)
266. 109. - What needs to be done
267. 110. - Direct link to the platform
268. 111. - Folder reference: `[folder](Applications/{Company}/{Role}_{ID}/)`
269. 112. - Tracker reference: `Tracker: data/tracker-active.json -> {Month} -> "{company}"`
270. 113. 
271. 114. ### Rule 6: Validate JSON after every tracker edit
272. 115. ```
273. 116. python -c "import json; json.load(open('data/tracker-active.json', encoding='utf-8-sig')); print('valid')"
274. 117. python -c "import json; json.load(open('data/tracker-closed.json', encoding='utf-8-sig')); print('valid')"
275. 118. ```
276. 119. 
277. 120. ### Rule 7: Export pages before taking action
278. 121. Use `window.__UMD.extractFullPage()` to export any page that contains important new information (assessment details, interview scheduling, etc.) before clicking any buttons.
279. 122. 
280. 123. ### Rule 8: Set LinkedIn viewport on every new tab
281. 124. Before navigating to any LinkedIn page:
282. 125. ```
283. 126. mcp_cdp-browser_emulate -> viewport 1920x1080, tabId: <your tab>
284. 127. ```
285. 128. 
286. 129. ### Rule 9: Post-session cleanup
287. 130. After completing all checks:
288. 131. 1. Run `python scripts/cleanup_formatting.py` to fix mojibake/formatting
289. 132. 2. Validate all tracker JSONs
290. 133. 3. Run `python scripts/check_stale.py` to identify stale applications
291. 134. 4. Update data/manual-tasks.md - add new items, mark completed items as DONE
292. 135. 5. Log any issues to `.temp/subagent-issues-log-{YYYYMMDD}.md`
293. 136. 6. Reset viewport on any tabs modified: `mcp_cdp-browser_emulate -> clearViewport` or close the tabs
294. 137.    - If user reports pages look zoomed/wrong: Ctrl+0 resets zoom, or close the tab
295. 138. 
296. 139. ### Rule 10: Human language
297. 140. All notes, emails, and replies must use `-` not `—`, `->` not `→`, straight quotes. Short sentences. No AI-sounding phrases.
298. 141. 
299. 142. ---
300. 143. 
301. 144. ## CHECKING WORKFLOW - STEP BY STEP
302. 145. 
303. 146. ### Phase 0: Preparation
304. 147. 
305. 148. 1. Read `candidate/current-status.json` - note email, phone, current experience
306. 149. 2. Read `data/tracker-active.json` - note all active applications, their statuses, dates
307. 150. 3. Read `data/manual-tasks.md` - note what was previously flagged
308. 151. 4. Group active applications by status for reference:
309. 152.    - `applied` - waiting for any response
310. 153.    - `in-progress` - incomplete forms, need action
311. 154.    - `shortlisted` - company showed interest
312. 155.    - `interview` - interview scheduled
313. 156.    - `replied` - in conversation
314. 157.    - `assignment` - take-home work
315. 158.    - `assessment-completed` - waiting for results
316. 159. 
317. 160. ### Phase 1: Gmail Check (t.ushara0901@gmail.com)
318. 161. 
319. 162. **Setup:**
320. 163. 1. Create a new browser tab via `mcp_cdp-browser_tabs` -> create
321. 164. 2. Navigate to `https://mail.google.com/mail/u/1/#inbox`
322. 165. 3. Wait for page to load fully
323. 166. 4. VERIFY the URL contains `/mail/u/1/` - if it shows u/0, navigate again explicitly
324. 167. 
325. 168. **Check inbox:**
326. 169. 1. Take a page snapshot to see all visible emails
327. 170. 2. For EACH unread email (or recent email from last few days):
328. 171.    a. Click on it to open it
329. 172.    b. Read the FULL email content (use `window.__UMD.extractFullPage()` to capture it)
330. 173.    c. Determine what kind of response it is:
331. 174.       - **Rejection email** -> keywords: "unfortunately", "moved forward with other candidates", "not a fit", "position has been filled"
332. 175.       - **Interview invite** -> keywords: "interview", "schedule a call", "next round", "meet the team"
333. 176.       - **Assessment/test** -> keywords: "coding challenge", "assessment", "test link", "HackerRank", "HackerEarth", "complete by"
334. 177.       - **Recruiter contact** -> keywords: "reviewed your profile", "interested in your background", "opportunity"
335. 178.       - **OTP/verification** -> keywords: "verify", "OTP", "confirmation code"
336. 179.       - **Application confirmation** -> keywords: "application received", "thank you for applying"
337. 180.    d. Match the email to a tracker entry (search by company name, role title)
338. 181.    e. Take the appropriate action (see Phase 5: Processing Responses)
339. 182.    f. Go back to inbox
340. 183. 
341. 184. **Check labels:**
342. 185. 1. Navigate to each label, especially "Dot 1" (if it exists)
343. 186. 2. Check for any emails that might have been auto-filtered away from inbox
344. 187. 3. Process any found emails same as above
345. 188. 
346. 189. **Check spam/promotions:**
347. 190. 1. Briefly check spam and promotions tabs for misfiltered recruiter emails
348. 191. 2. If found, process same as above
349. 192. 
350. 193. ### Phase 2: LinkedIn Check
351. 194. 
352. 195. **Setup:**
353. 196. 1. Create a new browser tab via `mcp_cdp-browser_tabs` -> create
354. 197. 2. Set viewport 1920x1080: `mcp_cdp-browser_emulate` with tabId
355. 198. 3. Navigate to `https://www.linkedin.com/notifications/`
356. 199. 4. Wait for load
357. 200.
358. ````
359. 
360. </details>
361.

375. 
376. ````
377. 200. 
378. 201. **Check notifications:**
379. 202. 1. Take snapshot, read all visible notifications
380. 203. 2. Look for:
381. 204.    - "Your application was viewed" -> update tracker note, change status to "shortlisted" if not already
382. 205.    - "Your resume was downloaded" -> update tracker note, change status to "shortlisted"
383. 206.    - "{Person} viewed your profile" -> check if related to an application
384. 207.    - "Interview scheduled" / "Assessment" notifications
385. 208.    - "Unfortunately" / rejection notifications
386. 209. 3. For each relevant notification, match to tracker entry and update
387. 210. 
388. 211. **Check messages:**
389. 212. 1. Navigate to `https://www.linkedin.com/messaging/`
390. 213. 2. Wait for load, take snapshot
391. 214. 3. For EACH unread or recent conversation:
392. 215.    a. Click on it using JS execute: `document.querySelector('SELECTOR').click()` (not CDP interact - LinkedIn Ember.js doesn't respond to CDP clicks on message items)
393. 216.    b. Read the full conversation
394. 217.    c. Look for recruiter messages, interview details, assessment links
395. 218.    d. Match to tracker entry and update
396. 219.    e. If a reply is needed, draft it following human language rules
397. 220. 
398. 221. **Check Easy Apply status (optional, time permitting):**
399. 222. 1. Navigate to `https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/tracker/applied/`
400. 223. 2. Check for any status updates on submitted applications
401. 224. 
402. 225. ### Phase 3: Hirist Check
403. 226. 
404. 227. **Setup:**
405. 228. 1. Use an existing Hirist tab or create a new one
406. 229. 2. Navigate to `https://www.hirist.tech/`
407. 230. 
408. 231. **Check notifications:**
409. 232. 1. Click the bell/notification icon
410. 233. 2. If a 403 overlay appears, dismiss it: `document.querySelectorAll('[class*="dialog"], [class*="modal"]').forEach(el => el.remove())`
411. 234. 3. Read each notification:
412. 235.    - "Shortlisted" -> update tracker to "shortlisted"
413. 236.    - "Not Suitable" -> update tracker to "rejected", move to Closed
414. 237.    - "Profile Viewed" -> add note to tracker
415. 238.    - "Interview" -> update tracker to "interview", add to data/manual-tasks.md
416. 239. 4. DO NOT try to navigate to /myApplications (returns 404)
417. 240. 
418. 241. **Check chat messages:**
419. 242. 1. If hirist has a chat/messaging feature visible, check for recruiter messages
420. 243. 2. Capture recruiter names and their companies
421. 244. 
422. 245. ### Phase 4: Other Platforms
423. 246. 
424. 247. **FoundIT (foundit.in):**
425. 248. 1. Check email for FoundIT notifications (they primarily communicate via email)
426. 249. 2. If logged in, check the dashboard for any status updates
427. 250. 
428. 251. **Turing (work.turing.com):**
429. 252. 1. Navigate to `https://work.turing.com/dashboard/applications`
430. 253. 2. Check for any new assessment invitations or status changes
431. 254. 3. Cross-reference with existing Turing entries in tracker
432. 255. 
433. 256. **Mercor (work.mercor.com):**
434. 257. 1. Navigate to `https://work.mercor.com/explore`
435. 258. 2. Check for interview invitations or step completions
436. 259. 3. Cross-reference with existing Mercor entries in tracker
437. 260. 
438. 261. **GoodSpace AI:**
439. 262. 1. Check email for GoodSpace notifications
440. 263. 2. Navigate to `https://goodspace.ai` if needed for status updates
441. 264. 
442. 265. **Mindrift/Toloka:**
443. 266. 1. Check email for Mindrift communications
444. 267. 2. Navigate to `https://app.mindrift.ai` for any new assessment availability
445. 268. 
446. 269. **Spotter.ai / Teamtailor:**
447. 270. 1. Check email for magic link login and assessment invitations
448. 271. 2. Assessment links EXPIRE - check deadlines carefully
449. 272. 3. Format: GitHub repo + hosted project + Loom video, typically 4 days max
450. 273. 
451. 274. **RemoteOK (remoteok.com):**
452. 275. 1. Check email for replies to email-based applications (search by company name from RemoteOK apps)
453. 276. 2. No in-platform messaging - all communication is via email
454. 277. 3. Cross-reference any company replies with tracker entries that have `platform: "remoteok"` or `platform: "remoteok -> ..."`
455. 278. 
456. 279. ### Phase 5: Processing Responses
457. 280. 
458. 281. For EACH response found, perform ALL of these steps:
459. 282. 
460. 283. **If REJECTION:**
461. 284. 1. Update tracker entry: change `status` to `"rejected"`
462. 285. 2. Move entry from `data/tracker-active.json` to `data/tracker-closed.json` under the same month key
463. 286. 3. Remove entry from Active file
464. 287. 4. Move application folder from `Applications/{Company}/` to `Applications/.archive/{Company}/`
465. 288. 5. Update `folderPath` in the Closed entry
466. 289. 6. If item exists in data/manual-tasks.md, mark as DONE or remove
467. 290. 7. Add timeline entry to Notes.md in the application folder: `| {date} | Rejection received |`
468. 291. 
469. 292. **If INTERVIEW INVITE:**
470. 293. 1. Update tracker entry: change `status` to `"interview"`
471. 294. 2. Add note with date, time, interviewer name if available
472. 295. 3. Add to data/manual-tasks.md under ASSESSMENTS or MONITORING with:
473. 296.    - Status, what needs to be done
474. 297.    - Link to scheduling page
475. 298.    - Folder and tracker references
476. 299. 4. Update Notes.md timeline: `| {date} | Interview invite received |`
477. 300. 5. Export the interview email/notification for reference
478. 301. 
479. 302. **If ASSESSMENT/TEST:**
480. 303. 1. Update tracker entry: change `status` to `"assignment"` or keep current
481. 304. 2. Add to data/manual-tasks.md under ASSESSMENTS with:
482. 305.    - Assessment type (coding, aptitude, writing, etc.)
483. 306.    - Time limit if any
484. 307.    - Platform (HackerRank, HackerEarth, Turing, custom)
485. 308.    - Direct link
486. 309.    - Deadline if specified
487. 310. 3. Update Notes.md timeline: `| {date} | Assessment link received |`
488. 311. 4. Export the assessment invitation email
489. 312. 
490. 313. **If SHORTLISTED / APPLICATION VIEWED:**
491. 314. 1. Update tracker entry: change `status` to `"shortlisted"`
492. 315. 2. Add note: "Application viewed on {date}" or "Resume downloaded on {date}"
493. 316. 3. Consider sending follow-up to hiring manager if visible
494. 317. 4. Update Notes.md timeline
495. 318. 
496. 319. **If RECRUITER REPLY:**
497. 320. 1. Update tracker entry: change `status` to `"replied"`
498. 321. 2. Add note with key details from the conversation
499. 322. 3. If reply needed, draft and send using human language rules
500. 323. 4. Add to data/manual-tasks.md under MONITORING
`````

571. 374. 
572. 375. ## FOLLOW-UP EMAIL TEMPLATES
573. 376. 
574. 377. When sending follow-up emails to recruiters/hiring managers:
575. 378. 
576. 379. **Initial follow-up (7-14 days after applying, no response):**
577. 380. ```
578. 381. Subject: Following up - {Role} application
579. 382. 
580. 383. Hi {Name},
581. 384. 
582. 385. I applied for the {Role} position at {Company} on {date}. Wanted to check in on the status. I have {experience} in {relevant skills} and am particularly interested in {specific thing about the role/company}.
583. 386. 
584. 387. Happy to share more details or jump on a call.
585. 388. 
586. 389. Best,
587. 390. Tushar Agarwal
588. 391. +91 8670263451
589. 392. ```
590. 393. 
591. 394. **After interview/assessment (3-5 days, no response):**
592. 395. ```
593. 396. Subject: Thank you - {Role} interview follow-up
594. 397. 
595. 398. Hi {Name},
596. 399. 
597. 400. Thank you for the {interview type} on {date}. I enjoyed discussing {specific topic from interview}. Looking forward to hearing about next steps.
598. 401. 
599. 402. Best,
600. 403. Tushar Agarwal
601. 404. ```
602. 405. 
603. 406. Rules for follow-up emails:
604. 407. - ALWAYS send from `mail.google.com/mail/u/1/` (t.ushara0901@gmail.com)
605. 408. - Use `-` not `—`, straight quotes, short sentences
606. 409. - No generic phrases like "I am writing to express my keen interest"
607. 410. - Reference something specific about the role or conversation
608. 411. - Keep it under 5 sentences
609. 412. 
610. 413. ---
611. 414. 
612. 415. ## LINKEDIN OUTREACH MESSAGE TEMPLATES
613. 416. 
614. 417. **Connection request to hiring manager:**
615. 418. ```
616. 419. Hi {Name}, I recently applied for the {Role} position at {Company}. With my background in {relevant experience}, I believe I could contribute well to your team. Would love to connect and learn more about the role.
617. 420. ```
618. 421. 
619. 422. **Follow-up message after connecting:**
620. 423. ```
621. 424. Thanks for connecting, {Name}. I applied for {Role} at {Company} on {date}. I have {experience} building {relevant projects/skills}. Would you be open to a brief chat about the role? Happy to share more about my work.
622. 425. ```
623. 426. 
624. 427. Rules:
625. 428. - Keep under 300 characters for connection notes
626. 429. - No "leverage", "synergy", or buzzwords
627. 430. - Mention something specific, not generic
628. ````

1137. ```
1138. 1. Hi Pat,
1139. 2. 
1140. 3. I saw the Vibe Coder listing on We Work Remotely and had to reach out - this is exactly how I work.
1141. 4. 
1142. 5. I am a Software Engineer at MAQ Software (2+ years) and I have gone all-in on agentic-first workflows. My most relevant work:
1143. 6. 
1144. 7. 1. Built a CDP Browser Automation MCP Server - a Chrome DevTools Protocol based automation tool I use daily. It drives real browser sessions through Claude, handles tab management, form filling, page exports, the works. Fully production-grade, not a demo.
1145. 8. 
1146. 9. 2. Built a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server at MAQ Software for integrating AI assistants into our project infrastructure. Designed the architecture to handle 120+ operations within AI context limits using nested tool patterns. The whole team uses it daily.
1147. 10. 
1148. 11. 3. Built a Universal Markdown Copier (Tampermonkey userscript) - adds a floating button to any webpage, copies the entire page as clean Markdown. Handles articles, code blocks, tables, images, videos, Jupyter notebooks, math equations. I built it because nothing else worked right.
1149. 12. 
1150. 13. My stack: React, TypeScript, Next.js, Python, Node.js. I have worked with Supabase and event-driven architectures. I am comfortable with React Native (built HAMS in Flutter/Dart, similar mobile dev paradigm).
1151. 14. 
1152. 15. I use Claude Code extensively - probably 4-6 hours daily. I know what it gets right, where it hallucinates, and when to step in. About 70% of AI-generated code needs real back-and-forth to get working. That pattern-matching comes from experience.
1153. 16. 
1154. 17. GitHub: https://github.com/Tushar49
1155. 18. LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/tushar-agarwal-8aa137202/
1156. 19. Resume attached.
1157. 20. 
1158. 21. Would love to chat about Wonder Dog.
1159. 22. 
1160. 23. Tushar Agarwal
1161. 24. t.ushara0901@gmail.com | +91 8670263451
1162. 25.
1163. ```

1182. 1. # Wonder Dog - Vibe Coder Wanted
1183. 2. Source: We Work Remotely (https://weworkremotely.com/remote-jobs/wonder-dog-vibe-coder-wanted)
1184. 3. Posted: April 9, 2026
1185. 4. Apply before: May 8, 2026

1188. 7. Region: Anywhere in the World
1189. 8. Apply: pat@wonder.dog
1190. 9. 
1191. 10. ## Company
1192. 11. Wonder Dog is an AI-powered preventative health platform for dogs. Uses regular blood work, AI health dashboards, and personalized care plans to help dogs live longer, healthier lives. VC-backed, pre-seed, small elite team of second-time founders.
1193. 12. 
1194. 13. ## Role
1195. 14. Full-stack engineer who has gone all-in on agentic-first workflows. Let Claude Code rip. Let it write, refactor, and ship. But have the years of experience to review what it produces, catch what it misses, and pop into the IDE when it matters.
1196. 15. 
1197. 16. ## Stack
1198. 17. - React Native for mobile
1199. 18. - React/TypeScript/Next.js for web
1200. 19. - AI-native architecture with Claude, Supabase, Inngest
1201. 20. 
1202. 21. ## Compensation
1203. 22. Full-time role with salary + equity.
1204. 23. 
1205. 24. ## How to Apply
1206. 25. Send a short bio and examples of recent agentic-first work to pat@wonder.dog
1207. 26.
1208. ```

21.   "code": "\n// Extract job listings from Stripe careers\nconst jobs = [];\n// Try common selectors for job listings\nconst cards = document.querySelectorAll('[class*=\"JobListing\"], [class*=\"job-listing\"], [data-testid*=\"job\"], .JobsListings__result, a[href*=\"/jobs/\"]');\nif (cards.length > 0) {\n  for (const c of cards) {\n    const text = c.innerText?.trim()?.substring(0, 200);\n    if (text && text.length > 10) jobs.push(text);\n  }\n}\n// Fallback: get main content\nconst main = document.querySelector('main, [role=\"main\"], .content');\nconst bodyText = (main || document.body).innerText;\nreturn JSON.stringify({ title: document.title, url: window.location.href, cardCount: jobs.length, jobs: jobs.slice(0, 20), bodyPreview: bodyText.substring(0, 3000) });\n",
22.   "tabId": "03146012FBEAB9C34467FCB4D7EC49F5"
23. }

25. I can see Stripe has **Bengaluru roles**. Let me get the full list filtered to India/Bengaluru.

27. Ran Execute JavaScript 
28. Completed with input: {
29.   "action": "script",
30.   "code": "\n// Get all job rows from the table  \nconst rows = document.querySelectorAll('table tbody tr, [class*=\"JobsListings\"] tr, [role=\"row\"]');\nconst jobs = [];\nif (rows.length > 0) {\n  for (const r of rows) {\n    const cells = r.querySelectorAll('td, [role=\"cell\"]');\n    if (cells.length >= 2) {\n      const role = cells[0]?.textContent?.trim() || '';\n      const team = cells[1]?.textContent?.trim() || '';\n      const location = cells[2]?.textContent?.trim() || '';\n      const link = r.querySelector('a')?.getAttribute('href') || '';\n      if (role) jobs.push({ role, team, location, link });\n    }\n  }\n}\n// If no table rows, try the text approach\nif (jobs.length === 0) {\n  const bodyText = document.body.innerText;\n  const bengaluruIdx = bodyText.indexOf('Bengaluru');\n  if (bengaluruIdx >= 0) {\n    // Extract all lines mentioning Bengaluru\n    const lines = bodyText.split('\\n');\n    for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {\n      if (lines[i].includes('Bengaluru') || lines[i].includes('India')) {\n        // Get the role name from previous lines\n        const context = lines.slice(Math.max(0, i-3), i+1).join(' | ');\n        jobs.push({ context: context.substring(0, 200) });\n      }\n    }\n  }\n}\nreturn JSON.stringify({ rowCount: rows.length, jobCount: jobs.length, jobs: jobs.slice(0, 30) }, null, 2);\n",
31.   "tabId": "03146012FBEAB9C34467FCB4D7EC49F5"
32. }

113. ## Step 1 - Dedup + eligibility gate
114. For each Stripe role:

132. ## Step 3 - Tailored application package

140. ## Step 4 - Application execution readiness

142. - contact info

147. - availability / notice period if needed

309. ## Step 1 & 2 Results: Eligibility + Evaluation
310. 
311. All 4 Stripe Bengaluru roles fail the experience gate:
312. 
313. | # | Role | Required Exp | Your Bracket | Verdict | Reason |
314. |---|------|-------------|-------------|---------|--------|
315. | 1 | **SE, Core Technology** | **6-12+ yr** | max=3.75, absMax=4.75 | **SKIP** | 6yr min exceeds absoluteMax (4.75yr) by 1.25yr |
316. | 2 | **SE, Data & AI** | **4+ yr** | max=3.75, absMax=4.75 | **BORDERLINE** | Between max and absMax. Skill match partial (data pipelines yes, large-scale distributed systems limited) |
317. | 3 | **SE, Fee Insights** | **5+ yr** | max=3.75, absMax=4.75 | **SKIP** | 5yr exceeds absoluteMax (4.75yr) |
318. | 4 | **SE, Internal Systems** | **7-12 yr** | max=3.75, absMax=4.75 | **SKIP** | 7yr min exceeds absoluteMax by 2.25yr |
319. 
320. ### Detailed Analysis:
321. 
322. **#1 Core Technology (6-12+ yr):** Large-scale distributed systems (Compute, Networking, DocumentDB, Caching). Needs deep infra experience at massive scale. 6yr minimum is 1.25yr beyond absoluteMax. Clear skip.
323. 
324. **#2 Data & AI (4+ yr):** Data pipelines processing billions of events, canonical data warehouses. 4yr minimum falls within absoluteMax range - but the "apply only if perfect skill match" condition isn't met. Your experience is automation/internal tooling, not large-scale data engineering or distributed data systems. Partial overlap (Python, backend services) but missing core requirements (large-scale distributed systems, data warehousing at scale). This would be a stretch application.
325. 
326. **#3 Fee Insights (5+ yr):** Agentic systems, billing explainability, full-stack with AI agents. 5yr exceeds absoluteMax. Skip.
327. 
328. **#4 Internal Systems (7-12 yr):** API design, Ruby, Mongo/MySQL, financial tracking systems. 7yr minimum is way beyond bracket. Skip.
329. 
330. ### Decision
331. 
332. Per experience bracket rules:
333. - **Roles 1, 3, 4:** `req > absoluteMax` -> **SKIP** (no exceptions)
334. - **Role 2 (Data & AI):** `req` (4yr) is within absoluteMax (4.75yr) but skill match is not "perfect" - you don't have large-scale distributed systems or data pipeline experience at the scale described
335. 
336. **Recommendation:** All 4 should be skipped. Role #2 is the only borderline case, but the JD explicitly wants experience "delivering, extending, and maintaining large scale distributed systems" which is a domain gap.
337. 
338. Want me to:
339. 1. **Apply to #2 anyway** as a stretch (it's Stripe, worth trying even with a slim chance)?
340. 2. **Set up a job alert** for Stripe India to catch future junior/new-grad postings?
341. 3. **Move on** to scanning other companies for better-matched roles?

429. Channel is still hidden and all 7 options including Event Hub are still present. The DSO hasn't evaluated yet because this SSR was opened directly (no DSO init). The DSO only evaluates when opened via the SR form's "Add/Update Service Details" button which triggers generateServiceOptionMapping.
430. 
431. The getOptions() returns the attribute's metadata options, not the filtered runtime options after DSO. The DSO's removeOptionSetValues action physically removes options from the control at runtime — but that only happens when the full DSO pipeline runs.
432. 
433. This confirms that CDP testing for DSO behavior requires the proper navigation flow (Project → SR → Add/Update Service Details) which triggers DSO initialization. Direct SSR URL access bypasses DSO.

D365 mashtest environment is consistently too slow for CDP browser automation: - Page navigation: Times out at 30-60 seconds - Frame initialization: Takes 60-90+ seconds after navigation completes - Xrm API calls: Time out when frame hasn't loaded - Direct SSR URL access: Bypasses DSO — doesn't trigger generateServiceOptionMapping

458. What's blocked
459. The key verification (seeing Event Hub appear/disappear in the runtime dropdown after DSO evaluates) requires the full flow: SR → "Add/Update Service Details" → SSR. Each D365 page navigation takes 60-90s, and the multi-step flow would need 4-5 navigations = 5-7 minutes minimum. CDP tools time out long before that.
460. 
461. Recommendation
462. Manual browser testing is the path forward. The DSO data is verified correct across all 4 verification cycles. Open mashtest in a regular browser (already pre-warmed), create an EMD SR on a GCR project, open the SSR, and take manual screenshots of the channel dropdown. This takes 2-3 minutes manually.

<details>
<summary>100 lines</summary>

```
1. #!/usr/bin/env node
2. 
3. /**
4.  * CDP Browser Automation — MCP Server  v4.13.0
5.  *
6.  * 11 tools with 87+ sub-actions for full browser automation.
7.  * Features: stable element refs (backendNodeId), auto-waiting, incremental snapshots,
8.  * per-agent session isolation with tab locking, framework-aware inputs, modal guards,
9.  * human-like interaction mode, Chrome instance/profile management, JavaScript debugger
10.  * with breakpoints/stepping/call-stack inspection, resource overrides, DOM/event breakpoints,
11.  * auto-console-error reporting, connection health monitoring, download tracking,
12.  * cross-origin (OOP) iframe support via Target.setAutoAttach.
13.  *
14.  * Tools:
15.  *   tabs      — Tab lifecycle (list, find, new, close, activate, info)
16.  *   page      — Navigation, snapshot, screenshot, content, set_content, add_style, wait, PDF, dialog, inject
17.  *   interact  — Click, hover, type, fill, select, press, drag, scroll, upload, focus, check, tap
18.  *   execute   — JS eval, script, call-on-element
19.  *   observe   — Console, network, request body, performance metrics, downloads
20.  *   emulate   — Viewport, color, UA, geo, CPU, timezone, locale, vision, network, SSL, etc.
21.  *   storage   — Cookies, localStorage, indexedDB, cache, quota
22.  *   intercept — HTTP request interception, mocking, blocking via Fetch domain
23.  *   cleanup   — Disconnect sessions, clean temp files, status, list_sessions, session, reset
24.  *   browser   — Browser instance discovery (Chrome, Edge, Brave), profile listing, connection switching
25.  *   debug     — JS debugger (breakpoints, stepping, call stack), resource overrides, DOM/event breakpoints
26.  *
27.  * Setup:  chrome://flags/#enable-remote-debugging → Enabled → Relaunch (or edge://flags for Edge)
28.  *
29.  * Env vars:
30.  *   CDP_PORT              Browser debugging port            (default: 9222)
31.  *   CDP_HOST              Browser debugging host            (default: 127.0.0.1)
32.  *   CDP_TIMEOUT           Command timeout in ms             (default: 30000)
33.  *   CDP_SESSION_TTL       Agent session TTL in ms           (default: 300000)
34.  *   CDP_USER_DATA         Chrome User Data directory path
35.  *   CDP_PROFILE           Auto-connect to Chrome instance by name or path
36.  *   CDP_DEBUGGER_TIMEOUT  Debugger auto-resume timeout ms   (default: 30000)
37.  */
38. 
39. import { Server } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/index.js";
40. import { StdioServerTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/stdio.js";
41. import {
42.   ListToolsRequestSchema,
43.   CallToolRequestSchema,
44. } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/types.js";
45. import WebSocket from "ws";
46. import { randomUUID } from "crypto";
47. import { readFileSync, writeFileSync, mkdirSync, existsSync, unlinkSync, readdirSync, statSync } from "fs";
48. import { join, dirname } from "path";
49. import { fileURLToPath } from "url";
50. 
51. // ─── Config ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
52. 
53. const CDP_HOST = process.env.CDP_HOST || "127.0.0.1";
54. const CDP_PORT = process.env.CDP_PORT || "9222";
55. const CDP_TIMEOUT = parseInt(process.env.CDP_TIMEOUT) || 30000;
56. const SESSION_TTL = parseInt(process.env.CDP_SESSION_TTL) || 300000;
57. const LONG_TIMEOUT = 120_000;
58. const MAX_INLINE_LEN = 60_000;
59. const CDP_DEBUGGER_TIMEOUT = parseInt(process.env.CDP_DEBUGGER_TIMEOUT) || 30000;
60. const __filename = fileURLToPath(import.meta.url);
61. const __dirname = dirname(__filename);
62. const REPO_ROOT = dirname(__dirname); // up from MCP Server/ to repo root
63. const TEMP_DIR = process.env.CDP_TEMP_DIR || join(REPO_ROOT, ".temp");
64. 
65. // ─── Temp File Management ───────────────────────────────────────────
66. 
67. function ensureTempDir() {
68.   if (!existsSync(TEMP_DIR)) mkdirSync(TEMP_DIR, { recursive: true });
69. }
70. 
71. function writeTempFile(name, content, encoding = "utf8", sessionPrefix = null) {
72.   ensureTempDir();
73.   autoCleanupTempFiles();
74.   const fileName = sessionPrefix ? `${sessionPrefix.substring(0, 8)}_${name}` : name;
75.   const p = join(TEMP_DIR, fileName);
76.   writeFileSync(p, content, encoding);
77.   return p;
78. }
79. 
80. function cleanupTempFiles() {
81.   if (!existsSync(TEMP_DIR)) return 0;
82.   const files = readdirSync(TEMP_DIR);
83.   let count = 0;
84.   for (const f of files) {
85.     try { unlinkSync(join(TEMP_DIR, f)); count++; } catch { /* ok */ }
86.   }
87.   return count;
88. }
89. 
90. const MAX_TEMP_FILES = 50;
91. const MAX_TEMP_AGE_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000; // 30 minutes
92. 
93. function autoCleanupTempFiles() {
94.   if (!existsSync(TEMP_DIR)) return;
95.   const files = readdirSync(TEMP_DIR);
96.   if (files.length <= MAX_TEMP_FILES) return;
97.   // Sort by creation time and remove oldest
98.   const withStats = files.map(f => {
99.     const p = join(TEMP_DIR, f);
100.     try { return { path: p, mtime: statSync(p).mtimeMs }; } catch { return null; }
```

</details>

"code": "\nconst main = document.querySelector('main, [role=\"main\"], .content') || document.body;\nconst text = main.innerText;\n// Check salary/location/visa info\nconst salaryIdx = text.indexOf('Salary');\nconst visaIdx = text.indexOf('visa');\nconst relocIdx = text.indexOf('relocat');\nconst sponsorIdx = text.indexOf('sponsor');\nconst remoteIdx = text.indexOf('remote');\nconst caIdx = text.indexOf('California applicant');\nconst relevant = [];\nif (salaryIdx > 0) relevant.push('SALARY: ' + text.substring(salaryIdx, salaryIdx + 300));\nif (caIdx > 0) relevant.push('CA NOTICE: found');\nif (visaIdx > 0) relevant.push('VISA: ' + text.substring(Math.max(0,visaIdx-100), visaIdx + 100));\nif (sponsorIdx > 0) relevant.push('SPONSOR: ' + text.substring(Math.max(0,sponsorIdx-100), sponsorIdx + 100));\n\n// Get the full who section\nconst whoIdx = text.indexOf('Who you are');\nif (whoIdx > 0) relevant.push('FULL: ' + text.substring(whoIdx, whoIdx + 1500));\nreturn relevant.join('\\n\\n---\\n\\n');\n",
505.   "tabId": "03146012FBEAB9C34467FCB4D7EC49F5"
506. }

"code": "\n// Check for redirects, iframes (Greenhouse, Workday, etc), forms\nconst url = window.location.href;\nconst iframes = [...document.querySelectorAll('iframe')].map(f => ({ src: f.src, id: f.id, name: f.name }));\nconst forms = [...document.querySelectorAll('form')].map(f => ({ action: f.action, method: f.method, id: f.id }));\nconst inputs = [...document.querySelectorAll('input, textarea, select')].slice(0, 20).map(i => ({\n  type: i.type, name: i.name || i.id, placeholder: i.placeholder, label: i.labels?.[0]?.textContent?.trim()\n}));\nconst bodyPreview = document.body.innerText.substring(0, 2000);\nreturn JSON.stringify({ url, iframeCount: iframes.length, iframes: iframes.slice(0, 5), formCount: forms.length, forms, inputCount: inputs.length, inputs, bodyPreview }, null, 2);\n",

"code": "\n// Try to access the Greenhouse iframe content\nconst iframe = document.getElementById('grnhse_iframe');\nif (iframe) {\n  try {\n    const iframeDoc = iframe.contentDocument || iframe.contentWindow.document;\n    const inputs = [...iframeDoc.querySelectorAll('input, textarea, select')].map(i => ({\n      type: i.type, name: i.name || i.id, placeholder: i.placeholder, \n      label: i.labels?.[0]?.textContent?.trim(),\n      required: i.required\n    }));\n    const forms = [...iframeDoc.querySelectorAll('form')].map(f => ({ action: f.action, id: f.id }));\n    return JSON.stringify({ crossOrigin: false, inputCount: inputs.length, inputs: inputs.slice(0, 25), forms });\n  } catch(e) {\n    return JSON.stringify({ crossOrigin: true, error: e.message, iframeSrc: iframe.src });\n  }\n}\nreturn 'no iframe found';\n",
608.   "tabId": "03146012FBEAB9C34467FCB4D7EC49F5"

<sub>⏱️ 21m 52s</sub>

### ✅ `task`

**Research Playwright MCP server**

<details>
<summary>Arguments</summary>

```json
{
  "name": "research-playwright-mcp",
  "agent_type": "general-purpose",
  "description": "Research Playwright MCP server",
  "mode": "background",
  "prompt": "Research the official Playwright MCP server (microsoft/playwright-mcp) thoroughly and create a detailed research document.\n\nYour goal: Create a file at `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\PlaywrightMCP.md` with a THOROUGH research document.\n\n## What to research:\n1. **Architecture**: How is Playwright MCP structured? What files, what modules? How does it organize tools?\n2. **Tool Design**: What tools does it expose? How are they designed? What parameters? Look at the actual tool names and descriptions from the GitHub repo.\n3. **Browser Management**: How does it manage browser lifecycle? Does it launch its own browser or connect to existing? How does it handle profiles/contexts?\n4. **Form Filling**: How does it handle form filling? What makes it so good at filling any field type - dropdowns, comboboxes, checkboxes, file uploads, React-based inputs?\n5. **Snapshot System**: How does it do accessibility snapshots? How does it keep them small/readable? What's the ref system?\n6. **Error Handling**: How does it handle stale elements, timeouts, navigation?\n7. **Session Management**: How does it manage sessions, tabs, contexts?\n8. **Performance**: What makes it fast? Lazy loading? Connection pooling?\n9. **Testing**: How is it tested?\n\n## How to research:\n- Use `github-mcp-server-get_file_contents` to read from `microsoft/playwright-mcp` repo\n- Start with the root README, package.json, and src/ directory structure\n- Read the key source files that implement tools, browser management, snapshot\n- Use web_search if needed for additional context\n\n## Output format:\nCreate the file at `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\PlaywrightMCP.md` with sections for each topic. Be extremely detailed - include actual code patterns, tool schemas, architecture decisions. This will be used to completely restructure a competing CDP-based MCP server."
}
```

</details>

What files, what modules? How does it organize tools?\n2. **Tool Design**: What tools does it expose? How are they designed? Tool names, descriptions, parameters.\n3. **Browser Connection**: How does it connect to Chrome/Edge? How does it handle profiles? Does it auto-detect browsers?\n4. **Snapshot System**: How does it take snapshots? Format? Size management?\n5. **Interaction Model**: How does it handle clicks, typing, form filling? What's the uid/ref system?\n6. **Performance Features**: Lighthouse, performance tracing, heap snapshots\n7. **Page Management**: How does it manage pages/tabs?\n8. **Error Handling & Resilience**: How does it handle stale refs, timeouts, disconnections?\n9. **Session Management**: Does it have session isolation? Tab locking?\n\n## How to research:\n- Use `github-mcp-server-get_file_contents` to read from `ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp` repo (this is Google's official one)\n- Look at the README, package.json, src/ directory\n- Read key implementation files\n- Use web_search if needed\n\n## Output format:\nCreate the file at `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\ChromeDevToolsMCP.md` with detailed sections. Include actual tool names, parameters, architecture patterns. This will be used to restructure a competing CDP-based MCP server to be the best in class."
}

Agent is idle (waiting for messages). agent_id: research-chrome-devtools-mcp, agent_type: general-purpose, status: idle, description: Research Chrome DevTools MCP, elapsed: 323s, total_turns: 1

[Turn 0]
Research document created at `E:\Projects\CDP Browser Automation\.research\ChromeDevToolsMCP.md` (~37KB, 17 sections).

**Key findings:**
- **Architecture**: Puppeteer-based (not raw CDP), single McpContext, tool mutex serializes all calls
- **29 tools** across 9 categories, defined via `definePageTool`/`defineTool` with Zod schemas
- **uid system**: A11y tree snapshots with sequential integer IDs, `ElementHandle` resolution via `getElementByUid()`
- **Smart waiting**: `WaitForHelper` detects navigation + DOM stability via `MutationObserver`
- **No session isolation, no tab locking, no debugger, no request interception, no cookie management** — these are all differentiation opportunities for a competing server
```

</details>

**Summary of findings across 11+ browser MCP servers:**

- **Microsoft Playwright MCP** (~29K stars) dominates with a11y-snapshot-first approach
- **mcp-chrome** (11K stars) fastest-growing — Chrome extension reusing your real browser
- **Playwriter** (3.4K stars) — radical single-tool design with full Playwright API

**Key takeaways for your project:** Your CDP-direct approach with existing browser connection, debugger, request interception, and cookie management is a genuine competitive advantage. The main gaps to consider are a11y snapshot support (now table stakes), batch form filling, and token optimization for the 50+ tool schema.

Agent is idle (waiting for messages). agent_id: research-playwright-mcp, agent_type: general-purpose, status: idle, description: Research Playwright MCP server, elapsed: 497s, total_turns: 1

[Turn 0]
Research document created at `E:\Projects\CDP Browser Automation\.research\PlaywrightMCP.md` (~45KB, 12 detailed sections).

Key findings:
- **Thin wrapper**: `@playwright/mcp` delegates everything to `playwright-core`'s internal tools
- **`defineTool()`/`defineTabTool()`** pattern with automatic modal state blocking
- **Accessibility snapshots** via `page.ariaSnapshot({ mode: 'ai' })` with ref-based element targeting (`aria-ref=`)
- **`waitForCompletion()`** auto-tracks all requests during actions, waits for navigation/XHR/fetch
- **Lazy initialization** at every layer (server → backend → context → tab)
- **Capability-gated tools**: only ~20 core tools by default, others opt-in
- **Unified `browser_fill_form`** handles textbox/checkbox/radio/combobox/slider in one call
- **Modal state system** blocks all tools until dialog/file chooser is handled
```

</details>

1. # Playwright MCP Server — Deep Architecture Research
2. 
3. > **Source**: `microsoft/playwright-mcp` v0.0.70 (wrapper) + `microsoft/playwright` (core implementation)
4. > **Research Date**: July 2025
5. > **Purpose**: Reference document for restructuring a competing CDP-based MCP server
6. 
7. ---
8. 
9. ## Table of Contents
10. 
11. 1. [Repository Structure & Architecture](#1-repository-structure--architecture)
12. 2. [Tool Design System](#2-tool-design-system)
13. 3. [Browser Management & Lifecycle](#3-browser-management--lifecycle)
14. 4. [Snapshot System & Element Refs](#4-snapshot-system--element-refs)
15. 5. [Form Filling & Input Handling](#5-form-filling--input-handling)
16. 6. [Error Handling & Modal States](#6-error-handling--modal-states)
17. 7. [Session, Tab & Context Management](#7-session-tab--context-management)
18. 8. [Response System & Output Design](#8-response-system--output-design)
19. 9. [Performance & Lazy Initialization](#9-performance--lazy-initialization)
20. 10. [Complete Tool Catalog](#10-complete-tool-catalog)
21. 11. [Testing Infrastructure](#11-testing-infrastructure)
22. 12. [Key Design Decisions & Takeaways](#12-key-design-decisions--takeaways)
23. 
24. ---
25. 
26. ## 1. Repository Structure & Architecture
27. 
28. ### Monorepo Layout
29. 
30. ```
31. microsoft/playwright-mcp/          ← Thin npm wrapper repo
32. ├── packages/
33. │   ├── playwright-mcp/             ← @playwright/mcp npm package
34. │   │   ├── cli.js                  ← Entry point (delegates to playwright-core)
35. │   │   ├── index.js                ← Exports createConnection()
36. │   │   ├── package.json            ← Dependencies: playwright, playwright-core
37. │   │   └── src/README.md           ← "Source is in Playwright monorepo"
38. │   ├── extension/                  ← Chrome browser extension
39. │   └── playwright-cli-stub/        ← CLI stub package
40. └── package.json                    ← Root (workspaces: packages/*)
41. 
42. microsoft/playwright/               ← Actual source code lives here
43. └── packages/playwright-core/src/tools/
44.     ├── mcp/                        ← MCP server infrastructure
45.     │   ├── index.ts                ← createConnection() - main entry
46.     │   ├── browserFactory.ts       ← Browser launch/connect strategies
47.     │   ├── config.ts               ← Config resolution (CLI + env + file)
48.     │   ├── program.ts              ← CLI argument parsing
49.     │   ├── protocol.ts             ← Protocol version (currently 1)
50.     │   ├── extensionContextFactory.ts ← Browser extension connection
51.     │   ├── cdpRelay.ts             ← CDP relay for extension
52.     │   ├── log.ts                  ← Debug logging
53.     │   └── watchdog.ts             ← Process watchdog
54.     ├── backend/                    ← Tool implementations & core logic
55.     │   ├── browserBackend.ts       ← BrowserBackend class (tool dispatcher)
56.     │   ├── context.ts              ← Context class (browser context wrapper)
57.     │   ├── tab.ts                  ← Tab class (page wrapper)
58.     │   ├── tool.ts                 ← defineTool() / defineTabTool() helpers
59.     │   ├── tools.ts                ← Tool registry & filtering
60.     │   ├── response.ts             ← Response builder & serializer
61.     │   ├── snapshot.ts             ← Snapshot + click/hover/selectOption/drag
62.     │   ├── form.ts                 ← Form filling (browser_fill_form)
63.     │   ├── keyboard.ts             ← Type/press/pressSequentially
64.     │   ├── navigate.ts             ← Navigate/back/forward
65.     │   ├── tabs.ts                 ← Tab management (list/new/close/select)
66.     │   ├── evaluate.ts             ← JavaScript evaluation
67.     │   ├── runCode.ts              ← Run Playwright code snippets
68.     │   ├── screenshot.ts           ← Screenshot with auto-scaling
69.     │   ├── files.ts                ← File upload (fileChooser modal)
70.     │   ├── dialogs.ts              ← Dialog handling (alert/confirm/prompt)
71.     │   ├── network.ts              ← Network request listing
72.     │   ├── route.ts                ← Network mocking/routing
73.     │   ├── console.ts              ← Console message retrieval
74.     │   ├── cookies.ts              ← Cookie CRUD
75.     │   ├── storage.ts              ← Storage state save/restore
76.     │   ├── webstorage.ts           ← localStorage/sessionStorage
77.     │   ├── wait.ts                 ← Wait for text/time
78.     │   ├── common.ts               ← Close/resize browser
79.     │   ├── mouse.ts                ← Coordinate-based mouse (vision cap)
80.     │   ├── pdf.ts                  ← PDF generation (pdf cap)
````

</details>

1. # Chrome DevTools MCP Server — Deep Research Document
2. 
3. > **Repository:** [ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp](https://github.com/ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp)
4. > **Author:** Google LLC (Apache-2.0)
5. > **Version analyzed:** 0.21.0
6. > **NPM Package:** `chrome-devtools-mcp`
7. > **MCP Name:** `io.github.ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp`
8. 
9. ---
10. 
11. ## Table of Contents
12. 
13. 1. [Overview & Design Philosophy](#1-overview--design-philosophy)
14. 2. [Architecture](#2-architecture)
15. 3. [Tool System Design](#3-tool-system-design)
16. 4. [Complete Tool Reference](#4-complete-tool-reference)
17. 5. [Browser Connection & Launch](#5-browser-connection--launch)
18. 6. [Snapshot System (A11y Tree)](#6-snapshot-system-a11y-tree)
19. 7. [Interaction Model (uid System)](#7-interaction-model-uid-system)
20. 8. [Page Management](#8-page-management)
21. 9. [Performance & Tracing](#9-performance--tracing)
22. 10. [Network & Console Monitoring](#10-network--console-monitoring)
23. 11. [Emulation System](#11-emulation-system)
24. 12. [Error Handling & Resilience](#12-error-handling--resilience)
25. 13. [Session Management & Concurrency](#13-session-management--concurrency)
26. 14. [Slim Mode](#14-slim-mode)
27. 15. [Key Architectural Patterns](#15-key-architectural-patterns)
28. 16. [Strengths & Weaknesses](#16-strengths--weaknesses)
29. 17. [Opportunities for a Competing Server](#17-opportunities-for-a-competing-server)
30. 
31. ---
32. 
33. ## 1. Overview & Design Philosophy
34. 
35. Chrome DevTools MCP is Google's official MCP server that gives AI coding agents access to Chrome DevTools capabilities for browser automation, debugging, and performance analysis.
36. 
37. ### Design Principles (from `docs/design-principles.md`)
38. 
39. | Principle | Description |
40. |-----------|-------------|
41. | **Agent-Agnostic API** | Use MCP standard. Don't lock into one LLM. |
42. | **Token-Optimized** | Return semantic summaries, not raw JSON. "LCP was 3.2s" > 50K lines of JSON. |
43. | **Small, Deterministic Blocks** | Give agents composable tools (Click, Screenshot), not magic buttons. |
44. | **Self-Healing Errors** | Return actionable errors with context and potential fixes. |
45. | **Human-Agent Collaboration** | Output readable by machines (structured) AND humans (summaries). |
46. | **Progressive Complexity** | Simple by default, advanced optional arguments for power users. |
47. | **Reference over Value** | For heavy assets (screenshots, traces), return file paths, not raw data. |
48. 
49. ### Key Technology Stack
50. 
51. - **Runtime:** Node.js v20.19+ (TypeScript, built with `tsc` + Rollup for bundling)
52. - **Browser Automation:** Puppeteer (24.42.0) — NOT raw CDP
53. - **Performance:** Chrome DevTools Frontend (`chrome-devtools-frontend`) for trace parsing
54. - **Auditing:** Lighthouse (13.1.0) for accessibility/SEO/best-practices
55. - **MCP SDK:** `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` 1.29.0
56. - **Schema Validation:** Zod (via MCP SDK's bundled zod)
57. - **Telemetry:** Custom ClearcutLogger (Google's analytics)
58. 
59. ---
60. 
61. ## 2. Architecture
62. 
63. ### Directory Structure
64. 
65. ```
66. src/
67. ├── index.ts                    # Entry point — creates McpServer, registers tools
68. ├── browser.ts                  # Browser launch/connect logic (Puppeteer)
69. ├── McpContext.ts                # Central context object (27KB — largest file)
70. ├── McpPage.ts                  # Per-page state wrapper
71. ├── McpResponse.ts              # Response builder with token-aware formatting (35KB)
72. ├── SlimMcpResponse.ts          # Simplified response for slim mode
73. ├── PageCollector.ts            # Network/console data collection per page
74. ├── DevToolsConnectionAdapter.ts # Bridges Puppeteer CDP sessions → DevTools frontend
75. ├── DevtoolsUtils.ts            # DevTools universe manager
76. ├── HeapSnapshotManager.ts      # Heap snapshot processing
77. ├── WaitForHelper.ts            # Smart wait-after-action system
78. ├── Mutex.ts                    # Simple FIFO mutex for tool serialization
79. ├── logger.ts                   # Debug logger
80. ├── types.ts                    # Core type definitions
````

</details>

1. # Browser Automation MCP Servers — Comparative Research
2. 
3. > **Last Updated:** April 2026
4. > **Purpose:** Inform the design and positioning of `cdp-browser-mcp-server` by analyzing all significant competitors.
5. 
6. ---
7. 
8. ## Table of Contents
9. 
10. 1. [Market Overview](#market-overview)
11. 2. [Tier 1: Major Players (1,000+ Stars)](#tier-1-major-players)
12.    - [Microsoft Playwright MCP](#1-microsoft-playwright-mcp)
13.    - [hangwin/mcp-chrome](#2-hangwinmcp-chrome)
14.    - [executeautomation/mcp-playwright](#3-executeautomationmcp-playwright)
15.    - [remorses/playwriter](#4-remorsesplaywriter)
16.    - [browserbase/mcp-server-browserbase](#5-browserbasemcp-server-browserbase)
17.    - [jae-jae/fetcher-mcp](#6-jae-jaefetcher-mcp)
18. 3. [Tier 2: Notable Projects (100–1,000 Stars)](#tier-2-notable-projects)
19.    - [kontext-security/browser-use-mcp-server](#7-kontext-securitybrowser-use-mcp-server)
20.    - [merajmehrabi/puppeteer-mcp-server](#8-merajmehrabipuppeteer-mcp-server)
21.    - [ChromeDevTools/chrome-devtools-mcp](#9-chromedevtoolschrome-devtools-mcp)
22.    - [cloudflare/playwright-mcp](#10-cloudflareplaywright-mcp)
23.    - [OTA-Tech-AI/web-agent-protocol](#11-ota-tech-aiweb-agent-protocol)
24. 4. [Tier 3: Niche / Emerging](#tier-3-niche--emerging)
25. 5. [Comparison Matrix](#comparison-matrix)
26. 6. [Performance Benchmarks](#performance-benchmarks)
27. 7. [Common Patterns & Best Practices](#common-patterns--best-practices)
28. 8. [Tool Design Analysis](#tool-design-analysis)
29. 9. [Key Takeaways for cdp-browser-mcp-server](#key-takeaways-for-cdp-browser-mcp-server)
30. 
31. ---
32. 
33. ## Market Overview
34. 
35. The browser automation MCP space has exploded since late 2024. There are **300+ repositories** on GitHub related to browser MCP servers, but only ~10–15 have significant traction. The ecosystem breaks into distinct architectural camps:
36. 
37. | Architecture Approach | Examples | Pros | Cons |
38. |----------------------|----------|------|------|
39. | **Playwright-based** | Microsoft Playwright MCP, executeautomation, Playwriter | Mature API, cross-browser, a11y snapshots | Spawns new browser, no existing sessions |
40. | **Puppeteer-based** | merajmehrabi, modelcontextprotocol/servers | Node.js native, lightweight | Chrome-only, limited API surface |
41. | **CDP Direct** | chrome-devtools-mcp, cdp-browser-mcp-server | Low-level control, connects to existing browser | More complex, requires Chrome flags |
42. | **Chrome Extension** | mcp-chrome, Playwriter | Uses real browser with logins, no extra process | Requires extension install, Chrome-only |
43. | **Cloud-hosted** | Browserbase, Bright Data | Scalable, managed, no local browser | Latency, cost, requires API keys |
44. | **AI-mediated** | Browserbase (Stagehand), browser-use | Natural language actions | Extra LLM calls, slower, less predictable |
45. 
46. ---
47. 
48. ## Tier 1: Major Players
49. 
50. ### 1. Microsoft Playwright MCP
51. 
52. | Attribute | Detail |
53. |-----------|--------|
54. | **Repository** | `microsoft/playwright-mcp` |
55. | **Stars** | ~29,000+ (part of Playwright ecosystem) |
56. | **Language** | TypeScript |
57. | **License** | Apache 2.0 |
58. | **npm Package** | `@playwright/mcp` |
59. | **Status** | Official Microsoft project, actively maintained |
60. 
61. #### Architecture
62. - Built directly on Playwright's browser automation engine
63. - Spawns a new browser instance (Chromium/Firefox/WebKit)
64. - Core innovation: **uses accessibility tree snapshots instead of screenshots**
65. - Operates on structured data — no vision models needed
66. - Supports persistent user profiles, isolated contexts, and browser extension mode
67. 
68. #### Key Differentiators
69. - **Accessibility-first approach**: All interactions go through the a11y tree, making it deterministic and LLM-friendly
70. - **Snapshot-based navigation**: Returns structured accessibility snapshots (~5-20KB) instead of screenshots (~100KB+)
71. - **Ref-based element targeting**: Elements are identified by `ref` attributes from snapshots
72. - **Code generation**: Automatically generates Playwright TypeScript test code from interactions
73. - **Cross-browser**: Supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit
74. - **Browser Extension mode**: Can connect to a running browser via Chrome extension
75. - **CLI + SKILLS alternative**: Microsoft recommends CLI for coding agents (more token-efficient)
76. - **Config file support**: JSON configuration for complex setups
77. 
78. #### Tools (20+ tools)
79. 
80. **Core Automation:**
```

</details>

{
  "agent_type": "general-purpose",

  "prompt": "Create a complete restructure plan for the CDP Browser MCP Server. This is the most important document — it must be actionable, detailed, and comprehensive.\n\nRead ALL these files for full context:\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\PlaywrightMCP.md`\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\ChromeDevToolsMCP.md`\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\OtherBrowserMCPs.md`\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\UserIdentifiedIssues.md`\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\MCP Server\\package.json`\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\MCP Server\\server.js` (first 200 lines for architecture understanding)\n- `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\README.md`\n\nCreate TWO files:\n\n### File 1: `E:\\Projects\\CDP Browser Automation\\.research\\RestructurePlan.md`\nThe detailed technical plan with:\n\n## 1. Vision & Positioning\nWhat this server should become. Our unique value prop: connects to user's REAL browser (with their auth, cookies, profiles, extensions) while being as reliable and easy-to-use as Playwright. The \"power user's browser MCP.\"\n\n## 2. Architecture Redesign\nBreak the 254KB monolith into modules:\n```\nsrc/\n├── index.ts                 # Entry point, server setup\n├── config.ts                # Configuration & env vars\n├── connection/\n│   ├── browser-discovery.ts # Auto-detect Chrome/Edge/Brave\n│   ├── cdp-client.ts        # CDP WebSocket client\n│   ├── health-monitor.ts    # Connection health & auto-reconnect\n│   └── profile-manager.ts   # Profile-aware connections\n├── tools/\n│   ├── registry.ts          # Tool registration system\n│   ├── tabs.ts              # Tab lifecycle\n│   ├── page.ts              # Navigation, snapshot, content\n│   ├── interact.ts          # Click, type, fill, select\n│   ├── form.ts              # Smart form filling (NEW)\n│   ├── execute.ts           # JS evaluation\n│   ├── observe.ts           # Console, network, perf\n│   ├── emulate.ts           # Device emulation\n│   ├── storage.ts           # Cookies, storage\n│   ├── intercept.ts         # Request interception\n│   ├── debug.ts             # Debugger, breakpoints\n│   └── browser.ts           # Browser/profile management\n├── session/\n│   ├── session-manager.ts   # Agent session isolation\n│   ├── tab-ownership.ts     # Tab locking & cleanup\n│   └── ttl-manager.ts       # Session TTL with warnings\n├── snapshot/\n│   ├── accessibility.ts     # A11y tree capture\n│   ├── element-refs.ts      # Stable element reference system\n│   ├── incremental.ts       # Differential snapshots\n│   └── token-optimizer.ts   # Reduce snapshot size for LLMs\n├── utils/\n│   ├── wait.ts              # Smart waiting (navigation, selector, network)\n│   ├── retry.ts             # Auto-retry with backoff\n│   ├── temp-files.ts        # Temp file management\n│   └── error-handler.ts     # Actionable error messages\n└── types.ts                 # TypeScript type definitions\n```\n\n## 3. Critical Fixes (Phase 1 — Week 1-2)\nFix the top 10 user-identified issues:\n1. Auto-connect on startup (detect browser, connect automatically)\n2. Default cleanupStrategy to \"detach\" \n3. Fix fresh tab about:blank issue\n4. Stable element refs (don't invalidate between calls)\n5. Smart form filling for React comboboxes\n6. Increase default timeout for SPAs (60s)\n7. Reduce snapshot size with token optimization\n8. Auto-reconnect on browser disconnect\n9. Clear actionable error messages\n10. Console error filtering (don't show extension errors)\n\n## 4. Architecture Migration (Phase 2 — Week 3-4)\n- TypeScript migration plan\n- Module-by-module extraction from server.js\n- Testing strategy for each module\n- Backward compatibility plan\n\n## 5. New Features (Phase 3 — Week 5-8)\n- Smart form filling engine (handles all field types)\n- Profile-aware tab creation\n- Lazy initialization\n- Modal state system\n- Wait-for-completion tracking\n- Batch form filling\n- Smart retry for stale elements\n- Agent guidance in tool descriptions\n- Health dashboard\n\n## 6. Performance Optimization (Phase 4)\n- Lazy per-tab connections (already exists, improve)\n- Snapshot caching & diffing\n- Connection pooling\n- Startup time optimization\n- Token budget management\n\n## 7. Testing Plan\n- Unit tests per module\n- Integration tests with real browser\n- Competitive benchmark tests (same task, CDP vs Playwright, measure speed)\n- Form filling test suite (Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, LinkedIn)\n\n## 8. Migration Path\n- How to go from current server.js to new architecture\n- How to keep backward compatibility during migration\n- Versioning strategy (v5.0.0)\n\n### File 2: `C:\\Users\\LuC!F3R\\.copilot\\session-state\\fdb75998-adcc-446b-88fd-2f0abcc68808\\plan.md`\nA concise summary plan in the session folder.\n\nAfter creating both files, update the SQL todo:\n```sql\nUPDATE todos SET status = 'done' WHERE id = 'restructure-plan'\n```"
}
````

```
1. # CDP Browser MCP Server — Complete Restructure Plan
2. 
3. > **Version:** 5.0.0 (target)
4. > **Current:** v4.13.0 — single-file monolith (`server.js`, 254KB, 4,867 lines)
5. > **Author:** Tushar Agarwal
6. > **Date:** July 2025
7. > **Status:** PLAN — Not yet implemented
8. 
9. ---
10. 
11. ## Table of Contents
12. 
13. 1. [Vision & Positioning](#1-vision--positioning)
14. 2. [Architecture Redesign](#2-architecture-redesign)
15. 3. [Critical Fixes — Phase 1 (Week 1–2)](#3-critical-fixes--phase-1-week-12)
16. 4. [Architecture Migration — Phase 2 (Week 3–4)](#4-architecture-migration--phase-2-week-34)
17. 5. [New Features — Phase 3 (Week 5–8)](#5-new-features--phase-3-week-58)
18. 6. [Performance Optimization — Phase 4 (Week 9–10)](#6-performance-optimization--phase-4-week-910)
19. 7. [Testing Plan](#7-testing-plan)
20. 8. [Migration Path](#8-migration-path)
21. 9. [Risk Assessment](#9-risk-assessment)
22. 
23. ---
24. 
25. ## 1. Vision & Positioning
26. 
27. ### What This Server Should Become
28. 
29. **The power user's browser MCP** — the only server that connects to your REAL browser (with your auth, cookies, profiles, extensions) while being as reliable and easy-to-use as Playwright MCP.
30. 
31. ### The Unique Value Proposition
32. 
33. Every other browser MCP falls into one of two camps:
34. 
35. | Camp | Example | Limitation |
36. |------|---------|------------|
37. | **Fresh browser** | Playwright MCP, Puppeteer MCP | No cookies, no sessions, no extensions. Useless for real workflows. |
38. | **Existing browser** | mcp-chrome, Playwriter | Requires Chrome extension install. Limited API surface. |
39. 
40. **CDP Browser MCP Server is the only one that**:
41. 1. Connects to the user's **already-running** Chrome/Edge/Brave — with all auth, cookies, profiles, extensions intact
42. 2. Requires **zero extension installs** — just enable a Chrome flag
43. 3. Provides **87+ automation actions** across 11 tools — the most comprehensive tool surface of any browser MCP
44. 4. Offers **DevTools-level capabilities** no competitor has: JS debugger, request interception, resource overrides, DOM breakpoints, cookie CRUD, network throttling, HAR export
45. 5. Starts **instantly** — WebSocket connect in milliseconds, no browser launch, no binary download
46. 6. Has **2 dependencies** (`ws` + MCP SDK) — the smallest footprint in the ecosystem
47. 
48. ### Target Users
49. 
50. 1. **AI coding agents** (Copilot, Claude, Cursor) that need to interact with authenticated web apps
51. 2. **Power users** who want full browser control without re-authenticating everywhere
52. 3. **Enterprise teams** testing internal apps behind SSO (D365, SharePoint, Azure Portal)
53. 4. **Job seekers** automating applications across Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, LinkedIn
54. 
55. ### North Star Metrics
56. 
57. - **Zero-config startup**: Agent connects and works without any `browser.connect()` call
58. - **Single-call form filling**: Any form field (combobox, date picker, React select) works in ONE tool call
59. - **< 3s snapshot time**: Even for complex pages (Gmail, D365, LinkedIn)
60. - **< 5 stale ref errors per 100 interactions**: Stable element references
```

</details>
